summary: Gruber analyzes Apple's announced transition from PowerPC to Intel processors, examining why he was wrong to doubt it would happen. He had believed Apple couldn't emulate PowerPC software at acceptable speeds on Intel and wouldn't risk the Osborne Effect of pre-announcing a platform shift. Rosetta, Apple's dynamic binary translation technology, solved the first problem by allowing existing PowerPC apps to run on Intel Macs. The Osborne Effect remains a real concern but Apple is absorbing the hit from a position of financial strength, buoyed by iPod revenue. Gruber concludes the switch was a pragmatic choice: IBM couldn't deliver on its PowerPC roadmap, and Apple decided to act from strength rather than wait and be forced to act from weakness.
key_points: Rosetta's dynamic binary translation is the essential enabling technology for the Intel transition, acting as an 'unsung hero' that lets existing PowerPC apps run without user intervention
The Osborne Effect will hurt Apple's Mac sales in the short term, but Apple's financial position and iPod revenue make it survivable rather than existential
Apple chose to transition from a position of strength rather than wait and risk being forced into it from desperation, as IBM failed to deliver promised G5 speeds or a G5 PowerBook
Universal Binaries benefit both Intel and PowerPC Macs, meaning current Mac owners aren't being abandoned
Rosetta is far less comprehensive than the old 68K emulator — it handles only applications, not drivers or system extensions, and is all-or-nothing per process
money_quotes: Rosetta is not just a side note or afterthought; it is an essential component in the transition strategy. It is not going to be marketed with trumpets and banners. Rosetta's role is that of the unsung hero.
Apple is marching into this transition chin-first, and they're simply going to take their lumps.
I'm guessing for anyone genuinely familiar with the semi-conductor industry, it was as ludicrous as a proposal for Coca-Cola to start producing Pepsi.

The thinking seems to be: better to act now, from a position of strength, and absorb the costs of the transition while the company is doing well, rather than wait a few years and risk being forced to act from a position of weakness or desperation.
I think it boiled to a choice between two difficult options: either initiate a painful and expensive transition to Intel processors, or stick with PowerPC and fall behind.
This transition period is going to be hard on Apple, not hard on Mac users.
Unlike the Classic environment — which runs apps in a visual ghetto — most users may not even notice that a few of their apps are running under Rosetta.
key_insight: Apple's Intel transition was a calculated gamble from a position of strength — absorb the pain of platform migration now rather than be forced into it later when IBM's PowerPC roadmap fails to deliver.
